misc.title: ūüí°Lost in Translation: The official Viz English version skipped Imu's Maki is sad but inebitable. Oda wrote Omen and Maki at the same time in the panels in Japanese version. The connection between Hak
misc.text: ūüí°Lost in Translation: The official Viz English version skipped Imu's Maki is sad but inebitable. Oda wrote Omen and Maki at the same time in the panels in Japanese version. The connection between Haki and Maki is interesting! LO NA GO (@otaku_D_sa) #OnePiece1180 This chapter dropped a very strong hint, and this is where you realize the greatness of translation and quality because most One Piece fans completely overlooked this. There is a striking similarity between two words: Ť¶áśįó = Haki ť≠Ēśįó = Maki Unfortunately, Maki was translated as Omen, or a sinister foreboding. While that isn‚Äôt technically a linguistic mistake, since many English translators relied on standard meaning! It arguably erased one of ¬ęOda‚Äôs terrifying wordplays¬Ľ, something likely only native Japanese speakers would fully catch. Because there is a powerful parallel between the two words: Ť¶á (Ha) = dominance, supremacy, overwhelming power (in a positive sense) ť≠Ē (Ma) = demonic aura, darkness, cursed or ominous force And both share śįó (Ki) = spiritual energy, life force What if Haki was something Joy Boy developed as a counterforce against Imu‚Äôs Maki? That would explain how Joy Boy‚Äôs Haki was able to repel the Gorosei, wound Imu, and even make her gasp under the pressure of his Haki during the Egghead Arc. What if Joy Boy spread Haki among humans so they could resist not only Devil Fruit powers, but also Imu‚Äôs demonic abilities? And what if the highest form of Haki ‚ÄúConqueror‚Äôs Haki‚ÄĚ belongs only to certain bloodlines and races‚Ä¶ ‚Ä¶and that is why Imu seeks to purge them from the earth through extermination, in order to create his own world? ‚ÄĒ https://nitter.net/otaku_D_sa/status/2045400981303595378#m

items.text: ūüí°Lost in Translation: The official Viz English version skipped Imu's Maki is sad but inebitable. Oda wrote Omen and Maki at the same time in the panels in Japanese version. The connection between Haki and Maki is interesting! LO NA GO (@otaku_D_sa) #OnePiece1180 This chapter dropped a very strong hint, and this is where you realize the greatness of translation and quality because most One Piece fans completely overlooked this. There is a striking similarity between two words: Ť¶áśįó = Haki ť≠Ēśįó = Maki Unfortunately, Maki was translated as Omen, or a sinister foreboding. While that isn‚Äôt technically a linguistic mistake, since many English translators relied on standard meaning! It arguably erased one of ¬ęOda‚Äôs terrifying wordplays¬Ľ, something likely only native Japanese speakers would fully catch. Because there is a powerful parallel between the two words: Ť¶á (Ha) = dominance, supremacy, overwhelming power (in a positive sense) ť≠Ē (Ma) = demonic aura, darkness, cursed or ominous force And both share śįó (Ki) = spiritual energy, life force What if Haki was something Joy Boy developed as a counterforce against Imu‚Äôs Maki? That would explain how Joy Boy‚Äôs Haki was able to repel the Gorosei, wound Imu, and even make her gasp under the pressure of his Haki during the Egghead Arc. What if Joy Boy spread Haki among humans so they could resist not only Devil Fruit powers, but also Imu‚Äôs demonic abilities? And what if the highest form of Haki ‚ÄúConqueror‚Äôs Haki‚ÄĚ belongs only to certain bloodlines and races‚Ä¶ ‚Ä¶and that is why Imu seeks to purge them from the earth through extermination, in order to create his own world? ‚ÄĒ https://nitter.net/otaku_D_sa/status/2045400981303595378#m
